JavaScript Notes › Phase 1 — Foundations › Topic 03
Phase 1 · Topic 03

Hoisting

JS Core TypeScript Interview Topic Concept
01 — What is Hoisting?

JavaScript Processes Your Code Before Running It

Before JavaScript executes a single line of your code, the engine does a compilation pass — scanning through the file and registering all variable and function declarations. This registration is what people call hoisting.

The name comes from the mental image of declarations being "lifted" to the top of their scope. But nothing actually moves — it's just that declarations are processed first, before execution begins.

💡

Think of a chef who reads the whole recipe before starting to cook. JavaScript reads your whole file first — registering all declarations — then starts executing. The "reading" phase is hoisting. The "cooking" phase is execution. By the time line 1 runs, JS already knows about every declaration in the file.

Phase 1
Compile
JS scans the entire scope. Registers all var declarations (set to undefined). Registers all function declarations (full definition stored). Registers let and const (put in TDZ — unusable until their line).
Phase 2
Execute
JS runs your code line by line. var assignments happen here. let/const exit the TDZ when their declaration line is reached. Function expressions and arrow functions are assigned here too.
02 — var Hoisting

var is Hoisted and Initialised to undefined

When var is hoisted, the declaration is registered and given the value undefined. The assignment stays where you wrote it. This means you can technically reference a var variable before its line — but it will be undefined, not the value you assigned.

This causes subtle, hard-to-find bugs — which is the core reason var was replaced by let and const.

⚠ What you write
console.log(name); // ???
var name = "Arjun";
console.log(name); // "Arjun"
⚠ What JS actually runs
var name = undefined; // hoisted
console.log(name); // undefined 😱
name = "Arjun";     // assignment stays
console.log(name); // "Arjun"
⚡

This doesn't throw an error — it silently returns undefined. That's what makes var hoisting so dangerous. You access a variable before its assignment, expect a crash or warning, and instead get undefined — a value that quietly propagates through your code and causes bugs far from the source.

var Hoists to Function Scope — Not Block Scope

Remember from Topic 02 — var ignores block scope. When hoisted, it floats all the way up to the top of the containing function (or the global scope if not in a function). Let and const hoist to the top of their block.

function example() {
  console.log(x); // undefined — var hoisted to function top

  if (true) {
    var x = 10;   // var ignores the if-block
  }

  console.log(x); // 10 — x leaked out of the block
}

// JS saw this as:
function example() {
  var x;          // hoisted to function top
  console.log(x); // undefined
  if (true) { x = 10; }
  console.log(x); // 10
}
03 — The Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)

let and const Are Hoisted But Not Initialised

let and const are hoisted — the engine knows they exist. But unlike var, they are not initialised to undefined. Instead they enter the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) — a state where the variable is registered but completely inaccessible. Any attempt to read it before its declaration line throws a ReferenceError.

💡

The TDZ is like a parcel that's been dispatched (registered) but not yet delivered (initialised). It exists in the system, but you can't open it yet. The moment the declaration line executes, the parcel arrives — the TDZ ends and the variable becomes usable.

// The TDZ for 'score' starts here (top of block)

console.log(score); // ReferenceError: Cannot access 'score' before initialization
                     // ← TDZ is active — variable known but inaccessible

let score = 100;    // ← TDZ ends here. score is now 100

console.log(score); // 100 ✓
⚡

The TDZ error message is misleading. "Cannot access before initialization" makes it sound like the variable doesn't exist yet. It does exist — it's just in the dead zone. This is why people say let/const are "not hoisted" — they are hoisted, just not usably so. The distinction matters in interviews.

TDZ in Practice — A Subtle Trap

The TDZ isn't just about accessing a variable before its line in the same block. It also affects functions that close over variables — if the function is called while the TDZ is still active, you still get an error even though the function is defined after the variable's line in the source.

// TDZ trap with functions
function getCount() {
  return count; // references count from outer scope
}

getCount(); // ReferenceError — count is still in TDZ when called here

let count = 0;

getCount(); // 0 ✓ — TDZ over, count is now initialised
04 — Function Declaration Hoisting

Function Declarations Are Fully Hoisted — Definition and All

Unlike var which is hoisted as undefined, function declarations are hoisted completely — both the name and the full function body. This means you can call a function declaration anywhere in its scope, even before the line it's written on.

// Calling before the declaration — works fine!
greet("Arjun"); // "Hello, Arjun" ✓

function greet(name) {
  console.log("Hello, " + name);
}

// JS hoisted the whole function before executing any line
💡

Function declarations are the only thing in JavaScript that is completely available before its line. This is intentional — it lets you organise code so the main logic is at the top and helper functions are at the bottom, which is a readable style some developers prefer.

Function Expressions and Arrow Functions Are NOT Hoisted

This is a critical distinction. Only function declarations (using the function keyword as a statement) are fully hoisted. Function expressions (assigned to a variable) and arrow functions follow the hoisting rules of their variable — which means var gets undefined, and let/const get TDZ.

⚠ var function expression
sayHi(); // TypeError: sayHi is not a function
          // sayHi exists but it's undefined!

var sayHi = function() {
  console.log("hi");
};
⚠ const arrow function
sayHi(); // ReferenceError
          // sayHi is in TDZ

const sayHi = () => {
  console.log("hi");
};
Declaration type Hoisted? Initialised to Usable before line?
var x Yes undefined Yes — but undefined
let x Yes TDZ No — ReferenceError
const x Yes TDZ No — ReferenceError
function fn() {} Yes Full definition Yes — fully callable
var fn = function() {} Yes (as var) undefined No — TypeError (not a function)
const fn = () => {} Yes (as const) TDZ No — ReferenceError
05 — Class Hoisting

Classes Are Hoisted But Enter the TDZ — Like let

Class declarations are hoisted but not initialised — they enter the TDZ just like let and const. You cannot use a class before its declaration line. This is the right behaviour — using a class before it's defined would be a bug.

const p = new Person("Arjun"); // ReferenceError — Person is in TDZ

class Person {
  constructor(name) {
    this.name = name;
  }
}

const p2 = new Person("Arjun"); // ✓ works fine
💡

Classes behave like let, not like function declarations. Define your classes before you use them — always. This is a good habit regardless of hoisting, because it makes code readable top-to-bottom.

06 — Interview Q&A
Interview Questions you must be able to answer out loud
What is hoisting in JavaScript?
Before JavaScript executes code, the engine does a compilation pass where it registers all variable and function declarations. This registration process is called hoisting. It's not that declarations physically move — it's that they're processed before any code runs. The key consequence is that you can reference certain things before the line they appear on.
What is the difference between how var and let/const are hoisted?
var is hoisted and initialised to undefined — so you can reference it before its line without an error, but it will be undefined. let and const are also hoisted, but they enter the Temporal Dead Zone — they're registered but completely inaccessible. Trying to read them before their declaration line throws a ReferenceError.
What is the Temporal Dead Zone?
The TDZ is the period between the start of a block and the line where a let or const variable is declared. During this time, the variable has been registered by the engine but cannot be accessed — any attempt throws a ReferenceError. The TDZ ends the moment the declaration line is executed. It exists to catch the kind of bugs that var's silent undefined hoisting used to hide.
Are let and const hoisted?
Yes — but this is a nuanced answer. They are hoisted in the sense that the engine registers them during the compilation pass. But they are not initialised, so they're unusable — they sit in the TDZ. Many people say "let and const are not hoisted" as a shorthand for "they're not usably hoisted", but technically they are hoisted. An interviewer who asks this question is likely testing whether you know this distinction.
What is the difference between a function declaration and a function expression when it comes to hoisting?
A function declaration (function fn() {}) is fully hoisted — both its name and body — so it can be called anywhere in its scope, even before it appears. A function expression (const fn = function() {} or an arrow function) is assigned to a variable and follows that variable's hoisting rules. If it's var, calling it early gives a TypeError. If it's let or const, you get a ReferenceError.
Why does accessing a var before its line give undefined instead of a ReferenceError?
Because var is hoisted and initialised to undefined during the compilation phase. The variable exists — it just hasn't been assigned yet. This is exactly the dangerous behaviour that let and const were designed to fix. A ReferenceError is loud and obvious; undefined quietly propagates through your code and causes bugs far from the source.
🔷

TypeScript Layer — TypeScript doesn't change JavaScript's hoisting behaviour at runtime. What it does is catch hoisting-related mistakes at compile time — before you even run your code. In most cases, TypeScript makes hoisting bugs impossible to ship.

07 — TypeScript Catches Hoisting Mistakes at Compile Time

TypeScript Errors Before You Run Your Code

The TDZ errors you read about — those only show up at runtime in plain JavaScript. TypeScript's compiler performs its own static analysis and flags the same problems as compile-time errors, so you see them in your editor immediately, not when a user hits your code in production.

// TypeScript catches this before it runs
console.log(name); // TS Error: Block-scoped variable 'name' used before its declaration

let name: string = "Arjun";

// Same for function expressions
greet(); // TS Error: Variable 'greet' is used before being assigned

const greet = () => console.log("hi");
💡

This is one of TypeScript's biggest practical benefits. Hoisting bugs that take 30 minutes to debug in JavaScript are caught in 0 seconds in TypeScript — your editor underlines them in red the moment you type them. When you use let and const with TypeScript, hoisting effectively becomes a non-issue.

08 — Typing Hoisted Functions in TypeScript

Function Declarations Can Be Called Before They're Written — and TypeScript Knows Their Types

When you call a fully-hoisted function declaration before its line, TypeScript knows its parameter types and return type — because it performed the same compilation pass. The type safety is fully applied even when calling before the line.

// TypeScript fully type-checks this even though calculate is defined below
const result = calculate(10, 5);     // ✓ inferred: number
const bad    = calculate("10", 5);  // TS Error: Argument of type 'string'
                                        // not assignable to parameter of type 'number'

function calculate(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a + b;
}
💡

TypeScript's compilation pass covers the whole file — it resolves all declarations before checking types. So even if you call a function before it's written, TS knows its full signature. The hoisting model and the type model work together seamlessly.

Prefer const Arrow Functions in TypeScript

In real TypeScript codebases, most developers prefer const arrow functions over function declarations for most cases. This isn't about hoisting — it's about consistency, easier typing of generics, and avoiding accidental this issues. Function declarations are still used and valid — especially for named exports in modules.

// Both are fine — preference varies by team and codebase

// Function declaration — hoisted, slightly more verbose to type
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a + b;
}

// const arrow — not hoisted, but cleaner in many TS contexts
const add = (a: number, b: number): number => a + b;

// Typing a const function explicitly with a type alias
type AddFn = (a: number, b: number) => number;
const add: AddFn = (a, b) => a + b; // TS infers parameter types from AddFn
09 — TypeScript Interview Q&A
Interview TypeScript questions on this topic
How does TypeScript handle hoisting?
TypeScript doesn't change JavaScript's hoisting behaviour — the compiled output still hoists the same way. What TypeScript adds is compile-time detection of hoisting mistakes. If you try to use a let or const variable before its declaration, TypeScript flags it as an error immediately in your editor — before you ever run the code. This means most hoisting bugs are caught instantly rather than discovered at runtime.
Does TypeScript know the types of hoisted function declarations?
Yes. TypeScript performs its own full-file analysis pass before type-checking, so it resolves all function declarations first. If you call a function declaration before its line, TypeScript fully knows its parameter types and return type — and will type-check the call just as correctly as if the function were defined above the call site.
Why do many TypeScript developers prefer const arrow functions over function declarations?
A few reasons: arrow functions don't have their own this binding (which avoids a class of bugs), they're easier to type with explicit type aliases, and using const means the function can never be accidentally reassigned. Function declarations are still perfectly valid — especially as named module exports. Most real codebases mix both depending on context.
10 — Key Takeaways

JavaScript + TypeScript — What to Lock In

  • Hoisting is JavaScript's compilation pass — declarations are registered before any code runs. Nothing physically moves.
  • var is hoisted and initialised to undefined — accessible before its line, but silently undefined. This is the bug-causing behaviour.
  • let and const are hoisted but enter the TDZ — inaccessible before their line. Accessing them throws a ReferenceError. This is the safe behaviour.
  • The TDZ (Temporal Dead Zone) exists to make "used before defined" errors loud and obvious — unlike var's silent undefined.
  • Function declarations are fully hoisted — name and body — and are callable anywhere in their scope.
  • Function expressions and arrow functions follow their variable's hoisting rules. const/let = TDZ. var = undefined (TypeError when called).
  • Classes behave like let — hoisted into TDZ. Never instantiate a class before its declaration.
  • In TypeScript, hoisting bugs are caught at compile time — your editor flags them immediately so they never reach production.
  • In real TS codebases, prefer const arrow functions for most cases — no hoisting ambiguity, no accidental this, easily typed with aliases.
